jokes.text: Don't... don't forget to get me my, uh, banana.
jokes.setup: Bret is being completely ignored by a hostile fruit vendor
jokes.punchline: He politely reminds his friend about the banana amid clear hostility
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Bret's oblivious politeness in face of obvious discrimination. The mundane banana request amid escalating tension is very Flight of the Conchords.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Don't... don't forget to get me my, uh, banana.

jokes.text: Where are you from? - New Zealand. - Yeah, that's what I thought.
jokes.setup: Vendor asks seemingly innocent question about origin
jokes.punchline: His 'I knew it' response reveals immediate prejudice
jokes.explanation: Excellent setup/payoff structure. The casual prejudice reveal is both shocking and absurd. Great economy in the exchange.

dialogue.speaker: Fruit Vendor
dialogue.line: Where are you from?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: New Zealand.

dialogue.speaker: Fruit Vendor
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's what I thought.

jokes.text: I don't serve your kind here.
jokes.setup: Casual fruit shopping interaction
jokes.punchline: Vendor treats New Zealanders like a persecuted minority over fruit
jokes.explanation: Applying serious racial discrimination language to New Zealand origin is brilliantly absurd. Sets up the entire episode's premise.

dialogue.speaker: Fruit Vendor
dialogue.line: I don't serve your kind here.

jokes.text: Neila, can you go boil this in disinfectant?
jokes.setup: Vendor has already refused service
jokes.punchline: He treats the apple as contaminated by New Zealand touch
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of the absurd prejudice. The medical contamination angle pushes it into even more ridiculous territory.

dialogue.speaker: Fruit Vendor
dialogue.line: Neila, can you go boil this in disinfectant?

jokes.text: I don't want our customers complaining that our fruit tastes like New Zealanders.
jokes.setup: Vendor treating apple as contaminated
jokes.punchline: Implies New Zealanders have a distinct taste that transfers to fruit
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully specific and absurd line. The idea that nationality has a transferable taste is perfectly ridiculous. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Fruit Vendor
dialogue.line: I don't want our customers complaining that our fruit tastes like New Zealanders.

jokes.text: Are you picking on Bret again, Jemaine?
jokes.setup: Bret is upset about racism at fruit stand
jokes.punchline: Murray assumes it's internal band conflict, not external discrimination
jokes.explanation: Perfect Murray character moment - he completely misreads the situation and assumes band drama instead of actual discrimination.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Are you picking on Bret again, Jemaine?

jokes.text: You're hurting all of us when you bring down New Zealand, okay?
jokes.setup: Murray thinks Jemaine is insulting New Zealand
jokes.punchline: He makes it sound like Jemaine is damaging national morale
jokes.explanation: Murray's overblown sense of national responsibility and completely missing the point. Good character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You're hurting all of us when you bring down New Zealand, okay?

jokes.text: Tension in the band. Bret upset. All sorted now.
jokes.setup: Murray has completely misunderstood a racism incident
jokes.punchline: He summarizes it as minor band tension that's resolved
jokes.explanation: Perfect Murray moment - his managerial summarizing style applied to completely the wrong situation. Great economy and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Tension in the band. Bret upset. All sorted now.

jokes.text: Oh, great. This is my favorite box.
jokes.setup: Bret receives what appears to be an empty package
jokes.punchline: He expresses genuine enthusiasm for the empty box itself
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Bret's simple, easily pleased nature. The idea of having a 'favorite box' is wonderfully absurd and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Oh, great. This is my favorite box.

jokes.text: Nothing from your mom, Jemaine. So? I'm just saying, all right?
jokes.setup: Murray mentions Bret got something from his mom
jokes.punchline: Murray unnecessarily rubs in that Jemaine got nothing
jokes.explanation: Murray's tactless but innocent cruelty. His 'I'm just saying' defense makes it worse. Good cringe comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Nothing from your mom, Jemaine.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I'm just saying, all right?

jokes.text: That's not a DVD, by the way. Yes, it is. Dubbed video dub.
jokes.setup: Bret calls his video a DVD
jokes.punchline: Bret's confident but nonsensical technical terminology
jokes.explanation: Bret's confident ignorance of technology. 'Dubbed video dub' is wonderfully redundant and wrong. Good character work.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: That's not a DVD, by the way.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yes, it is. Dubbed video dub.

jokes.text: 25 years and it's already obsolete, that one. They move fast now, don't they?
jokes.setup: Murray talking about upgrading office technology
jokes.punchline: 25 years is not a short time for technology to become obsolete
jokes.explanation: Murray's complete disconnect from normal technology cycles. 25 years is ancient for computers, but he acts like it's surprisingly fast.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: 25 years and it's already obsolete, that one. They move fast now, don't they?

jokes.text: We're just having a band meeting... talking about upcoming gigs, that sort of thing. Yeah, there's none.
jokes.setup: Murray tries to impress Jessica by talking up their success
jokes.punchline: Jemaine immediately deflates it with blunt honesty
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup/payoff with Murray's pretense immediately destroyed. Great economy and timing. Jemaine's deadpan honesty undercuts Murray's posturing.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: We're just having a band meeting... talking about upcoming gigs, that sort of thing.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, there's none.

jokes.text: What about your wife? I'm separated. Are you? Are you? Yes!
jokes.setup: Murray declares love for another woman
jokes.punchline: Murray's increasingly agitated insistence that his separation is real
jokes.explanation: Murray's defensive reaction suggests this separation might be recent or not entirely real. Good escalation in his responses.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What about your wife?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I'm separated.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Are you?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Are you?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yes!

jokes.text: I moved in with you guys for a month. I was upset, remember? Oh, is that why you were there?
jokes.setup: Murray reminds them of his staying with them
jokes.punchline: Bret had no idea why Murray was living with them
jokes.explanation: Brilliant revelation that Bret was completely oblivious to Murray's emotional crisis. Perfect character moment showing their disconnection.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I moved in with you guys for a month. I was upset, remember?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Oh, is that why you were there?

jokes.text: You talked me down off the roof. Ah, yes yes. Remember? No.
jokes.setup: Murray reminds them of his suicidal moment
jokes.punchline: They still don't remember this apparently significant event
jokes.explanation: Escalates the absurdity that they forgot saving Murray's life. Jemaine's final 'No' is perfectly timed and brutal.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You talked me down off the roof.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Ah, yes yes.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Remember?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Albi, the Racist Dragon song
jokes.setup: Children's TV show about a dragon
jokes.punchline: The dragon is explicitly racist in a kids' show format
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly subversive parody. Taking heavy themes and putting them in children's TV format is inspired. Highly quotable and memorable.

dialogue.line: Albi, the Racist Dragon song

jokes.text: ...grumbled Albi quite racistly.
jokes.setup: Narrator describing dragon's dialogue
jokes.punchline: Treating 'racist' as an adverb describing manner of speech
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay. 'Quite racistly' as an adverb is both grammatically creative and absurdly casual about racism. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: TV Narrator
dialogue.line: grumbled Albi quite racistly.

jokes.text: Get your hand off my tail, you'll make it dirty.
jokes.setup: Heartwarming moment between characters
jokes.punchline: Dragon continues being racist even during emotional bonding
jokes.explanation: Perfect character consistency - even in a touching moment, Albi can't help being racist. Good tension between sentiment and bigotry.

dialogue.speaker: Albi
dialogue.line: Get your hand off my tail, you'll make it dirty.

jokes.text: And suddenly, Albi wasn't racist anymore.
jokes.setup: Complex emotional journey and realization
jokes.punchline: Racism cured instantly and inexplicably
jokes.explanation: Perfect parody of simplistic children's show morality. Complex social issues resolved magically. Great economy and structure.

dialogue.speaker: TV Narrator
dialogue.line: And suddenly, Albi wasn't racist anymore.

jokes.text: Are you crying over the kids' show? Yes. So?
jokes.setup: Emotional children's show ending
jokes.punchline: Bret openly admits to crying and is defensive about it
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bret's emotional openness and defensiveness. The 'So?' is perfectly defensive.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Are you crying over the kids' show?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: So?

jokes.text: there's a lot of 'prejudism' here
jokes.setup: Murray explaining American racism
jokes.punchline: Murray uses the wrong word 'prejudism' instead of 'prejudice'
jokes.explanation: Good malapropism showing Murray's slightly off vocabulary. 'Prejudism' sounds almost right but isn't. Character-consistent verbal mistake.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: there's a lot of 'prejudism' here

jokes.text: You know, the... the English and whatnot. Redcoats... the oppressors. We're not English...
jokes.setup: Murray explaining American prejudice against them
jokes.punchline: Murray has wrong understanding of New Zealand's relationship to England
jokes.explanation: Murray's confused understanding of colonial history and New Zealand's identity. Shows his managerial confidence in wrong information.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You know, the... the English and whatnot. Redcoats... the oppressors. We're not English...

jokes.text: you're pretty much the most disliked race in this whole country. Wh... wh... what about black people?
jokes.setup: Murray claims New Zealanders are most discriminated against
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's innocent question about actual racial discrimination
jokes.explanation: Uncomfortable but clever subversion of persecution complex. Jemaine's naive question highlights absurdity of Murray's claim.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: you're pretty much the most disliked race in this whole country.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Wh... wh... what about black people?

jokes.text: They don't like you either.
jokes.setup: Question about black people facing discrimination
jokes.punchline: Murray interprets it as 'do black people like New Zealanders'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection. Murray completely misses Jemaine's point about actual discrimination and makes it about personal dislike. Very clever.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: They don't like you either.

jokes.text: Yeah, but Dave, you're Indian. You hate us? Yeah. Sometimes. But you're our best friend. I know.
jokes.setup: Dave lists groups that hate New Zealanders including Indians
jokes.punchline: Dave admits he sometimes hates his best friends
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dave moment - his casual honesty about hating his friends sometimes. The matter-of-fact 'I know' is beautifully awkward. Great character writing.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, but Dave, you're Indian. You hate us?

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Yeah. Sometimes.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: But you're our best friend.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: I know.

jokes.text: You can't sit there, gotta go to the back.
jokes.setup: Normal bus interaction
jokes.punchline: Bus segregation applied to New Zealanders
jokes.explanation: Continues the absurd discrimination theme. Applying historical segregation to New Zealanders escalates the episode's central premise.

dialogue.speaker: Bus Driver
dialogue.line: You can't sit there, gotta go to the back.

jokes.text: There's a dog in the New Zealand section.
jokes.setup: Vendor points to special New Zealand section
jokes.punchline: The section is shared with dogs, indicating low status
jokes.explanation: Great visual gag that escalates the discrimination. Lumping New Zealanders with dogs is both absurd and pointed. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: There's a dog in the New Zealand section.

jokes.text: Well, it's for dogs from America and people from New Zealand.
jokes.setup: Bret points out there's a dog in their section
jokes.punchline: Vendor treats this as perfectly logical segregation policy
jokes.explanation: Perfectly matter-of-fact delivery of absurd policy. The vendor sees no problem with equating New Zealanders and American dogs.

dialogue.speaker: Fruit Vendor
dialogue.line: Well, it's for dogs from America and people from New Zealand.

jokes.text: How dare you call me a racist! He doesn't mean racist. He means xenophobic.
jokes.setup: Vendor is offended by racism accusation
jokes.punchline: Jemaine helpfully corrects to more precise form of bigotry
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion. Jemaine's helpful correction to more accurate bigotry term is perfectly in character and hilariously misses the point. Excellent craft.

dialogue.speaker: Fruit Vendor
dialogue.line: How dare you call me a racist!

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: He doesn't mean racist. He means xenophobic.

jokes.text: You want a brown delicious to the face?
jokes.setup: Argument about Red Delicious apple
jokes.punchline: Vendor threatens violence with brown apple variety pun
jokes.explanation: Decent apple variety pun as threat. Shows vendor's escalating aggression while staying fruit-themed. Solid but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Fruit Vendor
dialogue.line: You want a brown delicious to the face?

jokes.text: I don't. Bret was sort of driving this situation.
jokes.setup: Vendor asks if they want to make trouble
jokes.punchline: Jemaine throws Bret under the bus to avoid confrontation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jemaine cowardice. Immediately blaming Bret to avoid conflict is exactly his character. Good economy and truth to character.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I don't. Bret was sort of driving this situation.

jokes.text: You... are you counting in your head? Yeah. What are you up to? Seven. Oh.
jokes.setup: Vendor threatens to count to 10
jokes.punchline: Bret casually asks for progress update on the threat countdown
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist moment. Bret's polite curiosity about the threatening countdown is perfectly deadpan and character-consistent. Very funny structure.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: You... are you counting in your head?

dialogue.speaker: Fruit Vendor
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: What are you up to?

dialogue.speaker: Fruit Vendor
dialogue.line: Seven.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: Too Many Mutha'uckers song
jokes.setup: Confrontation with fruit vendor
jokes.punchline: Epic gangsta rap song about mundane fruit shopping grievances
jokes.explanation: Brilliant musical set piece. Turning minor consumer grievances into hardcore rap is inspired. The censored swearing and mundane subject matter create perfect comedy tension.

dialogue.line: Too Many Mutha'uckers song

jokes.text: I pay my mother... ucking rent fortnightly
jokes.setup: Hardcore rap about life struggles
jokes.punchline: Very polite 'fortnightly' payment schedule in gangsta context
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedy contrast. 'Fortnightly' is distinctly New Zealand/British formal language in aggressive American rap context. Great character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I pay my mother... ucking rent fortnightly

jokes.text: Then... uckin' pop an apple in his ass, yeah
jokes.setup: Fruit-based violent threats
jokes.punchline: Culminates in absurd and crude fruit insertion threat
jokes.explanation: Peak escalation of fruit violence concept. Crude but fits the gangsta rap parody. Relies somewhat on shock value.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Then... uckin' pop an apple in his ass, yeah

jokes.text: We're in the middle of a race war, Murray. What's that? Bad.
jokes.setup: Murray asks how things are going
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's casual description of race war, then deadpan 'bad' explanation
jokes.explanation: Great escalation from fruit shopping to 'race war.' The deadpan 'Bad' as clarification is perfectly understated. Good structure and character work.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: We're in the middle of a race war, Murray.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: What's that?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Bad.

jokes.text: It is on. Oh! Oh, it just comes on! That's weird.
jokes.setup: Murray claims he can't turn on his computer
jokes.punchline: Computer was already on, Murray didn't know basic operation
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy showing Murray's tech incompetence and need to create excuses to see Jessica. His wonder at automatic startup is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Jessica
dialogue.line: It is on.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Oh! Oh, it just comes on! That's weird.

jokes.text: I can't really put my emotions into... into words. So I've decided to use lyrics. Well, this... they're also words.
jokes.setup: Murray explains his songwriting approach
jokes.punchline: Jemaine points out lyrics are still words
jokes.explanation: Perfect logic fail from Murray. His distinction between emotions/words and lyrics is nonsensical. Jemaine's correction is perfectly timed.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I can't really put my emotions into... into words. So I've decided to use lyrics.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Well, this... they're also words.

jokes.text: # Hi. # I like it. Yeah? 'Hi'? Yep. Is that it? Yeah.
jokes.setup: Murray presents his love song
jokes.punchline: Entire song is just one word, Bret likes it anyway
jokes.explanation: Brilliant minimalism. Murray's one-word song and Bret's genuine approval is perfect. Amazing economy and character work. The exchange timing is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Hi.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I like it.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: 'Hi'?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yep.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Is that it?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah.

jokes.text: I don't want to make it too convoluted.
jokes.setup: Suggestion to add more words to one-word song
jokes.punchline: Murray thinks adding any words would be too complex
jokes.explanation: Perfect Murray logic. Treating a two-word song as overly complicated shows his complete lack of artistic awareness.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I don't want to make it too convoluted.

jokes.text: I like pie. I had a budgie but it died.
jokes.setup: Murray expands his love song
jokes.punchline: Non-sequitur about dead pet in romantic song
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Murray's terrible romantic instincts. Dead pet references in love songs are obviously inappropriate but he doesn't see it.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I like pie. I had a budgie but it died.

jokes.text: # Hot leggy blonde chick, got it going on # Wanna see you wearing that thong thong thong... # ...Panties on
jokes.setup: Helping Murray write appropriate workplace love song
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's suggestion is completely inappropriate for workplace
jokes.explanation: Decent escalation showing Jemaine's worse romantic instincts than Murray's. Relies on crude content more than clever structure.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: # Hot leggy blonde chick, got it going on # Wanna see you wearing that thong thong thong... # ...Panties on

jokes.text: it's inappropriate. I can't say that in the workplace, can I? Too much mumbling. Yeah, a lot of it was inaudible.
jokes.setup: Murray correctly identifies inappropriate workplace content
jokes.punchline: Murray and Bret think the problem was unclear delivery, not content
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection. They correctly identify workplace inappropriateness but completely miss why it was inappropriate. Perfect character logic.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: it's inappropriate. I can't say that in the workplace, can I?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Too much mumbling. Yeah, a lot of it was inaudible.

jokes.text: Ah, Jessica. That's her name.
jokes.setup: Murray's been calling her 'tech support woman'
jokes.punchline: Murray just learned her name after claiming to be in love
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment revealing Murray's shallow infatuation. Being in love without knowing her name exposes the superficial nature of his feelings.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Ah, Jessica. That's her name.

jokes.text: Do you have the new Gipsy Kings cassette? Let me check. Yes, we have it. I would like to reserve it. I'll put it aside for you. Excellent. Goodbye.
jokes.setup: Bret making a normal library call
jokes.punchline: Extremely formal and polite phone interaction for cassette reservation
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bret's polite, formal nature even in mundane interactions. The outdated cassette format adds period charm.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Do you have the new Gipsy Kings cassette?

dialogue.speaker: Library Worker
dialogue.line: Let me check. Yes, we have it.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I would like to reserve it.

dialogue.speaker: Library Worker
dialogue.line: I'll put it aside for you.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Excellent. Goodbye.

jokes.text: With telephones, you make the call.
jokes.setup: Bret finishing phone call
jokes.punchline: Profound observation about the obvious nature of telephone use
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bret moment. His earnest observation of the completely obvious is exactly his character. Great economy and deadpan delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: With telephones, you make the call.

jokes.text: Inject strychnine into a piece of his fruit. Then when a customer dies, the C.I.A. Will trace it back to the vendor and he's going straight to the big house... Alcatraz.
jokes.setup: Dave suggests revenge on fruit vendor
jokes.punchline: Elaborate murder plot with outdated prison reference
jokes.explanation: Good Dave escalation. His casual suggestion of murder and outdated Alcatraz reference fits his character's dramatic solutions to minor problems.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Inject strychnine into a piece of his fruit. Then when a customer dies, the C.I.A. Will trace it back to the vendor and he's going straight to the big house... Alcatraz.

jokes.text: sometimes what I do is I think mean thoughts about the person. How do they know? They don't know.
jokes.setup: Dave suggests subtle revenge
jokes.punchline: Dave's method is completely internal and ineffective
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist logic. Dave's revenge method that accomplishes nothing perfectly captures ineffectual passive-aggression. Jemaine's question highlights the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: sometimes what I do is I think mean thoughts about the person. How do they know? They don't know.

jokes.text: Say a comeback to him! Banana balls! You look like a... a something... like a banana balls.
jokes.setup: Dave encourages Bret to respond with insult
jokes.punchline: Bret's completely ineffective nonsensical insult attempt
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bret moment. His complete inability to insult anyone even when angry is perfectly in character. 'Banana balls' as an insult is wonderfully weak.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Say a comeback to him!

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Banana balls! You look like a... a something... like a banana balls.

jokes.text: What is the bird? What's that? That's the bird. Well, that bird doesn't have any wings. It's only got one leg.
jokes.setup: Dave teaches them to flip someone off
jokes.punchline: They interpret 'the bird' literally and critique it as poor bird representation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant literal interpretation comedy. Their complete misunderstanding of the gesture and critiquing it as unrealistic bird anatomy is perfectly absurd and character-consistent.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: What is the bird? What's that?

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: That's the bird.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Well, that bird doesn't have any wings. It's only got one leg.

jokes.text: try putting some wings on your bird. There we go. That's much nicer. But you don't wanna be nice.
jokes.setup: Bret trying to improve the bird gesture
jokes.punchline: Bret makes it more anatomically correct, missing the point of aggression
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of the bird misunderstanding. Bret's instinct to make things 'nicer' completely defeats the purpose. Great character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: try putting some wings on your bird. There we go. That's much nicer. But you don't wanna be nice.

jokes.text: It's not a fucking school play production. It's the bird.
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine making elaborate hand gestures
jokes.punchline: Dave's frustrated explanation of simple offensive gesture
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Dave's frustration. 'School play production' perfectly captures their overly elaborate approach to a simple gesture.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: It's not a fucking school play production. It's the bird.

jokes.text: Throw another shrimp on the barbie. Ride around on your kangaroos all day. No no no no. That's Australians.
jokes.setup: Vendor using Australian stereotypes
jokes.punchline: Jemaine and Bret patiently correct the geographic confusion
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the geographic confusion. Their patient correction of racist stereotypes being applied to wrong country is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Fruit Vendor
dialogue.line: Throw another shrimp on the barbie. Ride around on your kangaroos all day.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No no no no.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: That's Australians.

jokes.text: The accents are completely different. Th... they're like, 'Where's the car?' And we're like, 'Where's the car?'
jokes.setup: Jemaine explaining accent differences
jokes.punchline: His demonstration sounds exactly the same
jokes.explanation: Brilliant comedic moment. Jemaine's insistence on obvious differences while demonstrating identical sounds is perfect. Excellent economy and highly quotable. One of the episode's best jokes.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: The accents are completely different. Th... they're like, 'Where's the car?' And we're like, 'Where's the car?'

jokes.text: # Hi, leggy blonde... #
jokes.setup: Murray practicing his love song
jokes.punchline: Brief snippet of his expanded terrible song
jokes.explanation: Simple callback to earlier song development. Sets up the full musical number but doesn't add much on its own.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Hi, leggy blonde...

jokes.text: she's no longer here. Oh, that's fine. We'll do it in the morning. No, I mean she's finished installing the new computer system. She's not coming back.
jokes.setup: Murray assumes Jessica is just temporarily unavailable
jokes.punchline: Greg clarifies she's permanently gone, destroying Murray's hopes
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Murray's romantic disappointment. The gradual revelation that she's permanently gone is well-structured cringe comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: she's no longer here.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Oh, that's fine. We'll do it in the morning.

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: No, I mean she's finished installing the new computer system. She's not coming back.

jokes.text: Leggy Blonde song
jokes.setup: Murray's romantic disappointment
jokes.punchline: Full musical number incorporating all his terrible lyrical elements
jokes.explanation: Strong musical set piece that brings together all Murray's terrible romantic instincts into a cohesive song. Good callback integration and character work.
